title: Credit Card Velocity Checks
spl:
index=payments sourcetype="card:authorization"
| bin _time span=15m
| stats count, dc(merchant_id) as merchants, dc(auth_result) as outcomes by card_token, _time
| where count>=8 AND merchants>=4
| sort -count
description: Detects card testing and enumeration via rapid auth attempts across merchants or regions.
value: Detects card testing and enumeration via rapid auth attempts across merchants or regions.
implementation: Tokenize PANs; never log full track data. Thresholds tuned per BIN and channel. Feed declines into case management for issuer contact.
visualization: Table (hot cards), Line chart (auth attempts per BIN), Single value (unique cards in alert state).
knownFalsePositives: Velocity from digital-wallet testing, e-commerce promos, or re-try traffic after network blips; compare to issuer and channel baselines before blocking.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Payment processor TA, gateway logs.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype="card:authorization"`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Tokenize PANs; never log full track data. Thresholds tuned per BIN and channel. Feed declines into case management for issuer contact.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=payments sourcetype="card:authorization"
| bin _time span=15m
| stats count, dc(merchant_id) as merchants, dc(auth_result) as outcomes by card_token, _time
| where count>=8 AND merchants>=4
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Credit Card Velocity Checks** — Detects card testing and enumeration via rapid auth attempts across merchants or regions.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype="card:authorization"`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Payment processor TA, gateway logs. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: payments; **sourcetype**: card:authorization. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=payments, sourcetype="card:authorization". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by card_token, _time** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where count>=8 AND merchants>=4` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Run the search on recent production data, spot-check a handful of results against the source system or operational runbooks (cash operations, EHR, payment gateway, etc.), and adjust thresholds to match your seasonal patterns and test windows so teams are not flooded with noise.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (hot cards), Line chart (auth attempts per BIN), Single value (unique cards in alert state).
grandmaExplanation: In Financial Services, we pay close attention to "Credit Card Velocity Checks" in our day-to-day work so that fraud, waste, and compliance issues do not slip by quietly. We help teams focus on the few situations that need a person, not every routine blip in the data.
dataModelAcceleration: Not applicable for the primary search: it relies on application- and industry-specific log formats, not a normalized CIM data model. Optional mapping to CIM, if you adopt it, is a separate follow-on project.
